name: inject
summary: Inject is a command line network tool for crafting, injecting, and sniffing a wide range of network protocols. It is designed for users who need a robust tool for detailed network protocol analysis and manipulation, whether for network troubleshooting, testing, or educational purposes.

documentation.readme: Inject is a command line network tool for crafting, injecting, and sniffing a wide range of network protocols. It is designed for users who need a robust tool for detailed network protocol analysis and manipulation, whether for network troubleshooting, testing, or educational purposes.
usage_sections: Multiple Protocol Support : Inject handles Ethernet, ARP, IP, ICMP, TCP and UDP.
Network Packet Injecting : Create and send custom network packets on specified network interfaces.
Payload Integration: Load payload files into network packets.
Network Sniffing : Capture network packets with customizable filtering options to focus on specific network protocols.

arguments.name: -C
arguments.description: CLI flag '-C' accepted by the tool; behavior is implementation-defined and should be confirmed against the upstream README.

arguments.name: -K
arguments.description: CLI flag '-K' accepted by the tool; behavior is implementation-defined and should be confirmed against the upstream README.

arguments.name: -S
arguments.description: CLI flag '-S' accepted by the tool; behavior is implementation-defined and should be confirmed against the upstream README.

arguments.name: -T
arguments.description: CLI flag '-T' accepted by the tool; behavior is implementation-defined and should be confirmed against the upstream README.

arguments.name: --target
arguments.description: Target host, IP, MAC, or URL the tool operates on.

arguments.name: --port
arguments.description: Target port (default: service default).

arguments.name: --interface
arguments.description: Network interface to bind to (e.g. wlan0mon, eth0).

arguments.name: --output
arguments.description: Output file path for the report or capture.

arguments.name: --verbose
arguments.description: Increase log verbosity (repeatable for higher levels).

arguments.name: --threads
arguments.description: Number of concurrent workers.

arguments.name: --timeout
arguments.description: Per-operation timeout in seconds.

arguments.name: --rate
arguments.description: Packet / request rate limit (packets/sec).

arguments.name: --wordlist
arguments.description: Path to a wordlist (users, passwords, paths).

arguments.name: --proxy
arguments.description: HTTP or SOCKS proxy URL for outbound requests.

arguments.name: --user-agent
arguments.description: HTTP User-Agent header value.

arguments.name: --cookie
arguments.description: HTTP Cookie header value.

arguments.name: --header
arguments.description: Extra HTTP header in key:value form (repeatable).

arguments.name: --insecure
arguments.description: Skip TLS certificate verification (lab use only).

arguments.name: --json
arguments.description: Emit JSON output instead of human-readable text.

arguments.name: --no-color
arguments.description: Disable ANSI color in output.

arguments.name: --quiet
arguments.description: Suppress non-error output.

arguments.name: --dry-run
arguments.description: Print what would be done without making any changes.

documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'any' phase against surfaces [wifi_2_4_ghz, wifi_5_ghz, shell_linux] in the Wireless category. Fits when the engagement needs: Inject is a command line network tool for crafting, injecting, and sniffing a wide range of network protocols. It is designed for users who need a robust tool for detailed network protocol analysis and manipulation, whet.
documentation.why_to_use: Prefer this tool based on upstream description: Inject is a command line network tool for crafting, injecting, and sniffing a wide range of network protocols. It is designed for users who need a robust tool for detailed network protocol analysis and manipulation, whether for network troubleshooting, testing, or educational purposes.
documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: git clone https://github.com/fksvs/inject; git clone https://gitlab.com/fksvs/inject. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `inject eth -i wlp5s0 -K 00:11:22:33:44:5 -a payload.txt`. Further invocations from upstream: `inject arp -i wlp5s0 -K 00:11:22:33:44:55 -S $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -D $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -r 1`; `inject ip -S $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -D $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -T 48`. Inject is a command line network tool for crafting, injecting, and sniffing a wide range of network protocols. It is designed for users who need a robust tool for detailed network protocol analysis and manipulation, whether for network troubleshooting, testing, or educational purposes.

trust.warning: Attribution/index entry only. Audit provenance, code, releases and licence before use.

use_cases: When you need Wireless, use inject because Inject is a command line network tool for crafting, injecting, and sniffing a wide range of network protocols. It is designed for users who need a robust tool f.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `inject eth -i wlp5s0 -K 00:11:22:33:44:5 -a payload.txt` as documented upstream for inject.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `inject arp -i wlp5s0 -K 00:11:22:33:44:55 -S $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -D $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -r 1` as documented upstream for inject.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `inject ip -S $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -D $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -T 48` as documented upstream for inject.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `inject tcp -S $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -s 4444 -D $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -d 80 -f syn -a payload.txt` as documented upstream for inject.
When chaining Wireless tools, pair inject with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `inject --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
